coredata.dc:title: BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposites as sensitive and selective platform for fluorescence and RGB based detection of vitamin B12
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Duhan, Jyoti
Saini, Saloni
Kumar, Himanshu
Obrai, Sangeeta
coredata.dc:description: Here, a brand-new fluorescence BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposite for measuring Vitamin B12 has been created. The technique is based on the interaction between Vitamin B12 and graphitic nitride nanosheets modified with graphene quantum dots co-doped with boron and phosphorus. The developed nanocomposites have been characterized by using various techniques such as HR-TEM, SEM, EDS, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, Photoluminescence and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Different excitation wavelengths were used, with a 320 nm excitation wavelength and a 416 nm emission wavelength chosen for fluorometric detection. It examined how several factors, including the sample solution's pH, the length of the incubation period, and the impact of NaCl concentration, affected the intensity of the fluorescence. A linear standardization curve was validated in the Vitamin B12 range of 0–60 nM under the authorized conditions. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were 9.61 nM and 29.12 nM respectively. Using newly created nanocomposites, an RGB-based sensor for Vitamin B12 detection was also constructed. Using this probe, it was possible to determine the amount of Vitamin B12 in human urine samples with respectable outcomes and satisfactory recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposites
Quantum dots
Fluorescent
Smartphone sensors
Real samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Boron and phosphorous co–doped graphene quantum dots (BPGQD)
Detection of vitamin B12 using BPGQD/g-C3N4
Selectivity for sensing of vitamin B12

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of BPGQD/g-C3N4 (b) SEM image of BPGQD (c) SEM image of g-C3N4 (d) TEM images of BPGQD/g-C3N4 (e) HR-TEM image of BPGQD/g-C3N4 and inset shows the average size of BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposites (f) HR-TEM images of g-C3N4 and inset shows the interplanar distance.

(a) Full scan XPS spectra (b) High resolution XPS spectrum of B1s (c) High resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (d) High resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (e) High resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (f) High resolution XPS spectrum of P2p of BPGQD/g-C3N4.

(a) FT-IR bands of g-C3N4, BPGQD, BPGQD/g-C3N4. (b). Observed (Eobs) and corrected (Ecor) efficiency of BPGQD/g-C3N4 towards the Vitamin B12. Optimization parameters of synthesized BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposites on the fluorescence intensity (c) Effect of time (minutes) (d) Effect of time (days) (e) Effect of pH (f) Effect of Concentrations of NaCl.

(a) XRD spectra of fabricated BPGQD/g-C3N4 (b) Overlay of UV–Vis absorption spectra of Vit.B12 and emission spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4. (c) Excitation dependent emission behaviour of BPGQD/g-C3N4. (d) EDS spectrum of BPGQD/g-C3N4.

(a) Fluorescence quenching spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4 with the addition of Vitamin B12. (b) linear plot of I0/I versus conc. of Vitamin B12. (c) CIE plot of fluorescence spectra of Fig.4(a). (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4.

(a) RGB color-based detector for Vitamin B12 (b) Selectivity plot of BPGQD/g-C3N4 by using various interfering species (c) Shows the fluorescence lifetime of BPGQD/g-C3N4 and BPGQD/g-C3N4+Vitamin B12.
Pictorial representation of synthesis of g-C3N4, BPGQD and BPGQD/g-C3N4.
Parameters used to calculate IFE of Vitamin B12.
The Intra-day and Inter-day precision of the BPGQD/g-C3N4 for detection of Vitamin B12.
Real sample studies of Vitamin B12 using BPGQD/g-C3N4.

head.ce:title: BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposites as sensitive and selective platform for fluorescence and RGB based detection of vitamin B12

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Dr BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Dr BR Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a brand-new fluorescence BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposite for measuring Vitamin B12 has been created. The technique is based on the interaction between Vitamin B12 and graphitic nitride nanosheets modified with graphene quantum dots co-doped with boron and phosphorus. The developed nanocomposites have been characterized by using various techniques such as HR-TEM, SEM, EDS, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, Photoluminescence and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Different excitation wavelengths were used, with a 320 nm excitation wavelength and a 416 nm emission wavelength chosen for fluorometric detection. It examined how several factors, including the sample solution's pH, the length of the incubation period, and the impact of NaCl concentration, affected the intensity of the fluorescence. A linear standardization curve was validated in the Vitamin B12 range of 0–60 nM under the authorized conditions. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) were 9.61 nM and 29.12 nM respectively. Using newly created nanocomposites, an RGB-based sensor for Vitamin B12 detection was also constructed. Using this probe, it was possible to determine the amount of Vitamin B12 in human urine samples with respectable outcomes and satisfactory recoveries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Group B vitamins include Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin), an organic complex with cobalt ion in its assembly. This vitamin is necessary for cell growth and development as a coenzyme; deficiencies cause pernicious anemia, weariness, nausea, weakness and weight loss. Since the body cannot synthesis vitamin B, it is a necessary vitamin for human nutrition [1]. You must get this vitamin naturally from foods including dairy, eggs, fish, oysters, meat and fowl. Vitamin B12 is scarce in plant-based foods thus vegetarians and those who avoid red meat must supplement their diets with multivitamin pills, capsules and injections [2,3]. Vitamin B12 is present in certain concentrations in edible wild mushrooms [4]. Vitamin B12 has been distinctive medication for the treatment of Pernicious anemia disease. Deficiency of Vitamin B12 is a global public health concern. Vitamin B12 is a vital component for the human body's preservation of neurological functioning because it plays a character role in the creation of red blood cells, which is necessary for a healthy immune system and DNA synthesis [5,6]. The majority of the clinical signs were weak muscles, diarrhoea, lack of energy, which had a noteworthy impact on the lifespan of Anaemia's patients [7]. L-methylmalonyl-CoA mutase and methionine synthase both require Vitamin B12 as a cofactor. Homocysteine is converted to the necessary amino acid methionine by the enzyme methionine synthase. A whole blood count (CBC) and a Vitamin B12 blood test level are the tests used to identify Vitamin B12 insufficiency [8,9].

Numerous publications have documented a number of analytical methods for Vitamin B12 detection to date including electrochemical analysis, high-performance HPLC, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, microbiological assay [10–14]. However, there are a number of disadvantages to these methods, such as the requirement for costly equipment and skilled specialists, the necessity for a lengthy and laborious sample preparation process, the lack of a widely available standard. Furthermore, there are issues with the optical methods since they frequently employ expensive noble metals like titanium, gold and silver or heavy metal doped quantum dots to produce decent nanoparticles that are toxic and expensive respectively. The fluorescence based recognition strategy using non-metal doped quantum dots is receiving a lot of attention because of its relatively high sensitivity, low cost, simplicity of usage and visual confirmation.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoscale carbon-based particles that belong to the graphene family, which is a two-dimensional (2D) structure consisting of crystalline carbon one atom thick, linked together by sigma bonds. It possesses remarkable optical, chemical, structural, electrical, tunable electrochemiluminescence and photoluminescence capabilities. GQDs are utilized in the creation of extremely sensitive sensors because of their exceptional characteristics. GQDs were previously created via “top-down” and “bottom-up” techniques, just like another nanomaterials. Graphene, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes and so on can be utilized as starting resources for the top-down method. GQDs have drawn interest in the fields of biosensing, supercapacitors, sensors, light-emitting diodes and bioimaging because of their special features [15–18].

The heterogeneous/hybrid materials known as nanocomposites are created at the nanometric scale which when combined showed different properties than bulk components. It is discovered that their structures are more intricate than those of micro composites. Due to their heterogeneous nature, nanocomposites characteristics are influenced by the same elements that affect traditional composites: component characteristics, composition, structure and interfacial interactions. These particles have different atomic properties due to their size. This modification of the properties of nanoparticles is beneficial in many fields [19–21].

Here, we report the fabrication of a nanocomposite comprising g-C3N4 and co-doped graphene quantum dots with phosphorus and boron for the highly focused and sensitive sensing of Vitamin B12. To our knowledge, phosphorous and boron co-doped GQD and g-C3N4 based nanocomposites have not yet been synthesized. Therefore, we describe in this study the one-step calcination method that produced g-C3N4 and bottom-up approach for synthesis of BPGQD as well as how the sensory system responded to Vitamin B12. This research seeks to develop innovative nanocomposites combining BPGQD and g-C3N4, with a focus on their potential application in detecting Vitamin B12. The aim of this research is to create a novel BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposite, with a focus on their potential application in detecting Vitamin B12. Overall, this research has the potential to contribute to the development of advanced sensing platforms for detecting Vitamin B12, with possible applications in healthcare, food safety, and environmental monitoring. Our substance has the benefit of being synthetically made, highly stable and having a very low detection limit. The addition of Vitamin B12 caused a quick dampening of the fluorescence of BPGQD/g-C3N4. Additionally, we created a portable RGB-based sensor that detect Vitamin B12 using developed BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposite.

We bought melatonin, l-dopa and epinephrine from Sigma-Aldrich. LOBA India provided the following: urea, citric acid, boric acid, phosphoric acid, melamine, l-dopa, melatonin, alanine, urea, cystine, rutin, epinephrine, sodium chloride, potassium hydroxide, zinc sulphates, calcium chloride, uric acid, fructose, ascorbic acid, and vitamin B12. Fresh double-distilled water was utilized to produce the samples for all tests, dilutions and analyses and analytical-grade reagents were employed throughout.

The thermal polycondensation process was used to create the g-C3N4 powder. Two grams of melamine and 5 g of citric acid were placed in a lidded alumina crucible, which was heated in a muffle furnace for 4 h at a temperature of 300 °C at a rate of 2.5 °C per minute. Once the g-C3N4 powder cooled down to room temperature, it turned bright yellow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Boron and phosphorous co–doped graphene quantum dots (BPGQD)
ce:section.ce:para: BPGQD were synthesized by using our previously published work [22]. Dissolving 5g of citric acid in 10 ml of distilled water, the mixture was combined with 2g of boric acid and 3 ml of phosphoric acid. The combined solution was then stirred to 40 °C for 20 min using a magnetic stirrer. Additionally, pour the aforementioned solution into a crucible and heat it to 180 °C for 6 h in a muffle furnace. Once the mixture cool down to room temperature, the brown liquid solution was extracted. To get rid of large particles, the resultant solution was filtered, centrifuged at 12000 rpm, and then poured into a dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the produced BPGQD were placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for additional characterisation and further use.

BPGQD and g-C3N4 were mixed to create BPGQD/g-C3N4. 0.5 g g-C3N4 was first dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. Then, using ultrasonication for 0.5 h, the 2 ml BPGQD was distributed in an aqueous solution of g-C3N4. After that, the combined solution was heated for 3 h at 180 °C in a Teflon autoclave. The liquid dark solution was removed when the mixture reached room temperature. After filtering, the resulting solution was centrifugated at 12000 rpm to remove big particles. The final product was named as BPGQD/g-C3N4 as shown in Scheme 1 .

Utilizing a UV-VIS spectrophotometer, the absorption spectra were captured and Fluorescence Spectrometer was used to record the fluorescence spectra. The structural characterisation of produced BPGQD/g-C3N4 was done using a variety of instruments, including an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), FT-IR spectra, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The quenching process of the developed BPGQD/g-C3N4 were deliberate by fluorescence spectra and a smartphone was employed for the RGB color detection.

Freshly generated Vitamin B12 stock solution with concentrations of 10−6 M was prepared in distilled water. Next, make a 10−6 M BPGQD/g-C3N4 synthetic solution. Different concentrations of Vitamin B12 (3.3,6.6,9.7,60 nM) were added to the BPGQDs/g-C3N4 solution in order to detect Vitamin B12. The combined solutions were then allowed to stabilize for 5 min at room temperature before the fluorescence spectra were observed. Centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 20min was applied to human urine samples in order to detect Vitamin B12 in genuine samples. Clean buffer solution was replaced with 1 % human urine solution in the following sensing procedures. Following the addition of the BPGQD/g-C3N4 solution in 1 % human urine at different final Vitamin B12 concentrations (10, 20, 30 and 40 nM), the fluorescence spectra were similarly obtained.

FE-SEM and HR-TEM pictures of the produced samples are shown in Fig. 1 for analysis of their morphology and structure. SEM image of BPGQD/g-C3N4 prove the large spherical shapes particles and lies at nanoscale (Fig. 1a) The prepared BGPQD have a monodispersed, quasi-spherical form (Fig. 1b). The SEM image of g-C3N4 shows in the (Fig. 1c) that showed the sheet like structure. The results showed that BPGQD were present because certain condensed dark spots were consistently dispersed on the surface of g–C3N4 nanosheets. TEM and HR-TEM image shows that synthesized nanocomposites are spherical and size greater than graphene quantum dots that proves the aggregations of particles occurs during formation of nanocomposites (Fig. 1d and e) and average size of the synthesized nanocomposite was 13 nm shown in inset of Fig. 1e. HR-TEM images of g-C3N4 shows in Fig. 1f and inset of Fig. 1f shows the lattice fringe of g-C3N4 and calculated interplanar distance is 0.628 nm.

UV–Vis spectrum of BPGQD/g-C3N4 shows absorption peak at 280 and 312 nm (Fig. 5d) due to π to π* of CO/CC and n to π* of CN/CO transitions [23]. Furthermore, the aqueous BPGQD/g-C3N4 solution showed a strong green color under a 365 nm UV lamp, indicating that it was fluorescent. EDS analysis confirms the presence of boron, phosphorous, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen (Fig. 4d) illustrating the successful synthesis of BPGQD/g-C3N4. The various phase structures of the synthesized samples were identified and resolute using the XRD analysis One prominent peak, which signifies the (002) distinctive crystal plane of g-C3N4, may be plainly seen at 25° for pure g-C3N4 [24–26]. There is another rather minor peak at 12.6° that corresponds to g-C3N4's (100) crystal plane (Fig. 4a). The presence of the two distinctive peaks demonstrated the distinctive multilayer stacking of CN-based materials that resembles graphite and peaks at 20.6° were due to the graphene quantum dots.

The FT-IR bands of the prepared samples are provided in order to look into the structure variation following the coating of BPGQD/g-C3N4. The typical FT-IR spectra of the pristine g-C3N4, BPGQD and BPGQD/g-C3N4 are displayed in Fig. 3a. The triazine rings of g-C3N4 have a special breathing mode, which is indicated by the peak at 870 cm−1. The peak, which lies between 1250 and 1600 cm−1 [27,28], is thought to be caused by the stretching vibration modes of heterocycles C–N and CN. The peaks between 3200 and 3500 cm−1 are caused by the N–H groups, which are produced from.

uncondensed amino groups or residual hydrogen atoms bound to the margins of the graphite-like CN. The FT-IR spectra of BPGQD showed a peak at 1240 cm−1, 1670 cm−1 and 3300-3500 cm−1 were due to the C–OH, CO and O–H stretching vibrations respectively [29]. The samples do not show the distinctive peak at 1240 cm−1 pertaining to the C–OH of BPGQD, according to the FT-IR spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4. This is mostly caused by the low loading quantity of BPGQD and the severe stretching vibration of C–N. XPS spectra and the elements of BPGQD/g-C3N4 were inspected in Fig. 2
demonstrating the existence of boron, phosphorus, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. This allowed researchers to better understand the chemical states of the samples and analyse the close connection between BPGQD and g-C3N4. Fig. 2a revealed the full scan XPS spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4. High scan XPS spectra of B1s (Fig. 2b) showed four peaks at 188.3, 189.7, 190.2 and 192 eV were due to the BC2O, B–C, B–O and BCO2 respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectra of the produced samples are displayed in Fig. 2c the signals at 284.3 eV, 285.9 eV and 287.6 eV can be attributed to the (C–B), (C–O) bond and hybridized carbon of sp2 in an aromatic ring that contains nitrogen (N–CN) respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum for sample is shown in (Fig. 2d), where two significant species peaks are located at 398.2 eV were due to N–H bond and electrons from sp2-hybridized nitrogen atoms in NC are responsible for the signal peak at 399.4 eV. Three peaks observed in high scan survey of O1s at 530.1, 531.3 and 532.7 eV were due to C–O–C, CO, B–O respectively (Fig. 2e). P2p high scan spectra (Fig. 2f) revealed two peaks at 132.6 and at 134 eV were due to C–O–P and CPO3 respectively [30–33].

Variations in the pH of the solution have a substantial effect on intensity and play a crucial role in sensing. We achieved this by making different pH buffer solutions ranging from 1 to 13 and recording the fluorescence spectra at the different pH values indicated in (Fig. 3e). At a pH of 7–8, we saw the highest PL intensity. It is revealed that the BPGQD/g-C3N4 fluorescence intensity is considerably high in buffer solution with a pH of 8.0 [51]. As can be seen in (Fig. 4c), the emission spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4 were detected by variable different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 380 nm. We discovered that as the excitation wavelength enlarged from 280 to 320 nm, the emission intensity increased as well after that with increasing in excitation wavelength the fluorescent intensity decreased. As a result, the maximum excitation wavelength for BPGQD/g-C3N4 was 320 nm. As the incubation duration rised from 0 to 5 min following the adding of vitamin B12, the fluorescence intensity of BPGQD was dramatically lowered, and after 5 min, the changes were gradual (Fig. 3c). Thus, after 5 min, we made the decision to identify Vitamin B12. The synthesized BPGQD/g-C3N4 exhibit exceptional stability and highly fluorescent. As demonstrated in (Fig. 3f) the BPGQD/g-C3N4 fluorescence remained constant after the addition of high salt concentration that showed the low toxicity of fabricated sensors. At room temperature, the synthesized materials exhibit good stability for up to one months (Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of vitamin B12 using BPGQD/g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para:
Sensing studies were carried out using the fluorescence-based method to detect Vitamin B12 with high sensitivity. As Vitamin B12 content increased, a linear quenching of the BPGQD/g-C3N4 fluorescence intensity was detected. With the aid of a fluorescence quenching plot, the stern Volmer constant (KSv) can be computed using the formula
(1)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
[ Q ]
K sv
.
where [Q] is the concentration of Vitamin B12 and I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of BPGQD/g-C3N4 at 416 nm in the presence and absence of Vitamin B12, respectively [34,35]. It was discovered that the limits of detection, limit of quantification and stern volmer constant (Ksv) were respectively 9.61 nM, 29.12 nM, 21.25*106 M−1.

As mentioned in the experimental section, the detections were made by directly addition of different concentrations of Vitamin B12 to the BPGQD/g-C3N4 standard solution in order to streamline the process. The fluorescence fluctuations of nanocomposites that are dependent on Vitamin B12 content are shown in (Fig. 5a). It is discovered that when Vitamin B12 is added, the fluorescence intensity at 416 nm steadily decreases. Furthermore, as demonstrated by (Fig. 5b) repeatability, there is a strong linear correlation between the standardized fluorescence intensity and the Vitamin B12 content, which ranges from 0 to 60 nM and an R2 value of 0.9929. Based on 3.3σ/x (where x is the calibration cure slope and σ is the blank signal standard deviation (S/N = 3), the limit of detection (LOD) of 9.61 nM was determined. Fig. 5c shows the CIE plot of fluorescence quenching of BPGQD/g-C3N4 by the addition of Vitamin B12. Fig. 4b shows the overlay of UV–Vis absorption spectra of Vitamin B12 and emission spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4. IFE or Fluorescence Energy Resonance Transfer (FRET) are two commonly employed mechanisms to elucidate the quenching process resulting from overlapping UV absorption and fluorescence emission. The distance between the energy donor and acceptor must be under 10 nm for FRET to occur. The decrease in fluorescence intensity observed in the synthesized materials can be ascribed to hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between Vitamin B12 and BPGQD/g-C3N4. Various interactions between the quencher (Vitamin B12) and the fluorophore (BPGQD/g-C3N4) illustrate how the fluorescence intensity of BPGQD/g-C3N4 diminishes upon the addition of Vitamin B12. Fluorescence quenching denotes a sensing probe's reduction in fluorescence intensity due to diverse factors, including ground-state complex formation, energy transfer, molecular interactions, collisional quenching, and excited-state reactions. Hence, the detection mechanism of vitamin B12 with BPGQD/g-C3N4 is IFE. The initial fluorescence lifetime of BPGQD/g-C3N4 was 3.718 ns, and with the addition of vitamin B12, the fluorescence lifetime was 3.769 ns, as shown in Fig. 6 c. We observed no change in the fluorescence lifetime of BPGQD/g-C3N4 with the addition of vitamin B12, hence the detection mechanism was IFE. Our work's outcomes are contrasted with those of other documented fluorescence techniques. Table 1 illustrates how our suggested method may determine Vitamin B12 at a low detection limit and detect it over a wider concentration range. To assess its repeatability, the fluorescence variations of nanocomposites in the presence of Vitamin B12 at various intervals were also evaluated. The Quenching of fluorescent intensity of nanocomposites may be due to the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between Vitamin B12 and IFE mechanism BPGQD/g-C3N4.

It's intriguing to observe that the fluorescence emission spectra of BPGQD/g-C3N4 partially coincide with the absorption spectra of Vitamin B12 (Fig. 4b). This suggests that the fluorescence quenching of BPGQD/g-C3N4 by Vitamin B12 could be brought about by either IFE or FRET mechanisms [48,49]. Initially, the quenching mechanism of BPGQD/g-C3N4 by Vitamin B12 was presumed to originate from IFE. The IFE effect is adjusted using Eq. (1):
(2)
C F =
F c o r
F o b s
=
2.3 d A e x
1 −
10
− d A e x
10
g A e m
2.3 s A e m
1 −
10
− s A e m

The corrected factor, denoted as CF, Fobs observed fluorescence intensity and Fcor is the corrected fluorescence intensity, by eliminating Inner Filter Effect (IFE) from Fobs. The absorbance values at excitation wavelength (λex = 320 nm) and emission wavelength (λem = 416 nm), represented by Aex and Aem respectively. Additionally, the parameters s, g, and d, set at 0.10, 0.40, and 1.00 cm, respectively, are specified [5,50]. The computed results are presented in Table 2 . Furthermore, the quenching efficiency of observed (Eobs) and corrected (Ecor, after IFE removal) fluorescence intensities of BPGQD/g-C3N4 with varying concentrations of Vitamin B12 at 416 nm is depicted in Fig. 5a. It is evident that the IFE suppression efficiency for Vitamin B12 on BPGQD/g-C3N4 reaches up to 54 % of the total suppressed efficiency, indicating that the primary contribution to the suppressed efficiency stems from IFE as shown in Fig. 3b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity for sensing of vitamin B12
ce:section.ce:para: The detection method was used to validate how highly selective the newly created fluorescence sensing mechanism for Vitamin B12 is. To evaluate the proposed mechanism selectivity, several species were selected and each of them was subjected to the identical detection procedure. The degree to which Vitamin B12 could be distinguished from other species, such as l-dopa, melatonin, alanine, urea, cystine, rutin, epinephrine, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Ca2+, uric acid, fructose, ascorbic acid, sulphates, chlorides etc. was evaluated. All of the imported species were present in greater amounts than the Vitamin B12 that was added to the BPGQD/g-C3N4 solution. As (Fig. 6b) shows, no appreciable changes were observed for any other species. It demonstrates how highly selective the recently developed fluorescence sensing technique is for Vitamin B12.

The accuracy of imaging detection using portable devices and easily navigable smartphones surpasses that of visual detection using the human eye [42–44]. Software for an Android smartphone was utilized internally in the development of the BPGQD/g-C3N4 solution-based nano sensor. The smartphone displayed the findings of the Android app's simultaneous analysis of the image taken by the camera, which was done using software based on the RGB (red, green, and blue color space). The camera should be positioned as close to the sample container as possible (around 6 cm away) in order to capture the images without utilizing a flash. The gradual adding of Vitamin B12 to BPGQD/g-C3N4 caused a color shift that was noticeable to the unaided eye from dark blue to light blue. The limit of detection is 28.22 μM within linear range of conc. from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 6a). Afterwards, RGB profiling was employed to measure color quantifications utilizing the smartphone camera [45–47].

Vitamin B12 concentrations in human urine samples were measured using BPGQD/g-C3N4 as a fluorescence probe to confirm the accuracy and practicality of the fluorescence method by combining Vitamin B12 in different amounts that are known to work. Urine samples were used to create the spiked samples and different known quantities of Vitamin B12 were added.

The assay recoveries of spiking Vitamin B12 typically range from 96.1 % to 104.7 %, with an RSD of 0.753–2.731 %, as Table 4 illustrates. The results demonstrated that, Vitamin B12 may be found in human serum samples using this approach. To assess the reliability and precision of the BPGQD/g-C3N4 for detection of vitamin B12, both intra-day and inter-day detections were performed, and the respective results are presented in Table 3
. The data in Table 3 indicate that the relative error (RE) and relative standard deviations (RSD) were consistently below 5.20 % and 3.08 % respectively [48].

In conclusion, a novel fluorescent BPGQD/g-C3N4 nanocomposites were synthesized and used as a probe for the sensing of Vitamin B12 under different conditions. Fluorescence quenching could be observed at a wavelength of 416 nm upon adding Vitamin B12. In order to detect the amount of vitamin B12 in urine samples, a novel assay technique was created, assessed and used in this investigation. The procedure for determining vitamin B12 in samples that have been tampered with is straightforward, accurate, sensitive and has a wide recovery range. As far as we are aware, this is the first instance of BPGQD nanoparticles being integrated into graphitic nitride nanosheets. For the sensing of vitamin B12 in complex matrices, the technique showed good selectivity and repeatability (0.753–2.731 %, RSD), with limits of detection and quantification of 9.61 and 29.12 nM respectively. The RGB color detector software was also helps to detect Vitamin B12 in wide linear range (0–100 μM) with LODs of 28.22 μM.
There was no Funding.

Jyoti Duhan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Saloni Saini: Methodology, Data curation. Himanshu Kumar: Writing – review & editing. Sangeeta Obrai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author declare no competing interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nano RNA aptamer wire for analysis of Vitamin B12
Anal. Biochem.
